failure_surface: All target cells K6:K10 received identical formula structures that failed to incorporate row-specific values (item name and parameter column index), resulting in formulas referencing the same criteria ('A' for all items, column index 1 for all parameters) instead of dynamically adapting per row.
root_cause: The agent wrote formula templates to cells without properly interpolating Python variables (target_item, param_index) into the f-strings. When building multi-row Excel formulas via openpyxl, the agent failed to ensure each cell receives unique, context-aware formula content based on its row's input values.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before assigning any formula string to a worksheet cell, verify that all variables referenced in the formula (e.g., target item from column H, parameter type from column I, lookup date from column J) are interpolated into the f-string at write time—not just calculated once and reused across all rows.
evidence: Final verification shows identical formula structures in K6:K10: '=IFERROR(INDEX($C$3:$E$13,SMALL(IF(($B$3:$B$13="A")...))' — all contain hardcoded '"A"' regardless of row's actual item value (A, B, C)
All formulas end with ',1)' indicating constant parameter column index 1, but rows require indices 1, 3, 2, 3, 3 respectively based on column I values
Code shows second loop iterates row 6-11 but builds formula with uninterpolated 'target_item' variable and hardcoded ',1)' instead of {param_index}
Agent printed 'Formula applied' messages for each row before saving, masking the fact that identical content was written repeatedly

memory_items.title: Variable interpolation in per-row formula generation
memory_items.lesson: When generating distinct formulas for multiple cells in a loop, every variable that differs by row must be interpolated inside the f-string at assignment time, not captured in an earlier scope.
memory_items.generalization: For spreadsheet tasks requiring row-varying formulas, verify each cell's final formula content reflects its unique inputs (items, dates, parameters) by testing one iteration before scaling to all rows.

memory_items.title: Validate formula parameter columns dynamically
memory_items.lesson: Excel INDEX/MATCH formulas that select different columns based on user criteria must compute the column offset/column letter dynamically, not hardcoded.
memory_items.generalization: Always calculate and embed the result column index/letter within the formula string itself when criteria-driven parameter selection is required.

memory_items.title: Spot-check intermediate outputs for multi-step writes
memory_items.lesson: When writing formulas to multiple cells programmatically, verify a sample subset after assignment to detect if content varies correctly across rows.
memory_items.generalization: Implement sanity checks that compare cell contents before and after bulk formula operations to catch unintended duplication of formula templates.

edits.content:
## Variable Interpolation in Multi-Row Formulas

When generating distinct Excel formulas for multiple cells via Python:

- **Verify row-specific content:** Every formula assigned inside a loop must interpolate variables that differ by row (item names, parameter types, cell references) into the f-string at assignment time.
- **Avoid hardcoded values:** Do not capture criterion values outside the loop and reuse them; recalculate or re-interpolate for each cell.
- **Compute parameter columns dynamically:** If a formula returns different columns based on input criteria (e.g., Parameter 1/2/3 → C/D/E), calculate the column index inside the formula string construction.
- **Sanity check after assignment:** Before saving, spot-check multiple rows to confirm formulas vary correctly rather than duplicating identical content.

Example pattern:
```python
for row in start_row, end_row:
    target = ws.cell(row=row, col=H_col).value
    param_idx = {'Param 1': 1, 'Param 2': 2, 'Param 3': 3}[ws.cell(row=row, col=I_col).value]
    formula = f'=INDEX(...,{param_idx})'  # param_idx must be evaluated per row
    ws[f'K{row}'] = formula
```
